summary: Gruber responds to Tim Bray's suggestion that Apple should open source its bundled applications like Mail, Safari, iCal, and iChat. While acknowledging Bray's valid points — that the apps' competitive advantage lies in UI design rather than source code, and that the code would be valuable for developer education — Gruber argues Apple won't do it because these apps serve as upgrade incentives. New features in bundled apps are key selling points for each Mac OS X release, and open sourcing them would allow outside developers to backport features to older versions, undermining Apple's lucrative upgrade cycle. He frames this as the fundamental dichotomy between closed and open source development models.
key_points: Apple's bundled apps serve as major selling points for OS upgrades — open sourcing them would let developers backport new features to older versions, reducing upgrade incentive

Bray is correct that the apps' competitive strength lies in UI design, not source code — anyone can copy the best ideas just by looking at them — but that misses the real competitive threat: competition with Apple's own previous OS versions
WebKit being open source while Safari remains closed perfectly illustrates Apple's strategy: share the framework, keep the product
Open source software tends to improve in small steady increments while commercial software improves in large gulps designed to entice paid upgrades — fundamentally different development rhythms
The bundled apps transformed Mac OS X releases into events with midnight release parties, something the classic Mac OS never achieved
money_quotes: He overlooks another form of competition: existing versions of Mac OS X.
The role these apps play isn't just to make Mac OS X look good compared to Windows or Linux, but also to help make each new version of Mac OS X look better than the previous one.
This is one reason why Web Kit, the rendering framework, is open source, but Safari, the application, is not.
How many software products can you think of that have users lining up around the block for a midnight release party?

In short, releasing the source to these apps would be a risk. Not a risk with a catastrophic downside, but a risk nonetheless. And the potential upside — the best case scenario from Apple's perspective — wouldn't result in any additional sales.
While open source software is, by definition, eminently tweakable, it also, in general, is less likely to get to the point of being very cool and really good in the first place.

Open source software tends to improve in small, steady, frequent increments. Established commercial software tends to improve less frequently but in large gulps so as to entice users to pay for upgrades.
key_insight: Apple won't open source its bundled apps not because competitors might copy them, but because doing so would let developers backport new features to older OS versions, undermining Apple's most effective upgrade incentive.
